critique.summary: The article argues against expanding age verification systems across the internet as a means of child protection, warning that such systems shift the internet from open access to a permissioned model that undermines privacy and parental guardianship. It highlights the risks of centralized control, corporate lobbying influences, and the potential for these systems to evolve into broader identity verification layers beyond age checks.
mainPoints: Age verification is expanding beyond adult websites into mainstream services like social media and operating systems.
Age verification functions as an access control system rather than a simple child-safety feature, changing the internet's default from open to permissioned access.
Centralized age verification conflates content moderation with guardianship, which are distinct responsibilities best handled locally by parents and communities.
Age verification systems are vulnerable to easy bypass methods, making them ineffective and potentially a corporate data collection tool.
Lobbying and nonprofit grants have influenced US age-verification laws, benefiting companies profiting from user data.
The infrastructure for age verification can be repurposed for other forms of identity control, threatening broader privacy and freedom.
The article advocates for local content moderation and guardianship rather than centralized age verification layers.
It calls for regulation to focus on harmful recommendation systems and business models rather than on universal identification.
critique.truthfulness: The article presents a critical perspective on age verification systems, which is supported by documented expansions of such systems into various online platforms and proposals for OS-level age verification. The concerns about privacy, corporate lobbying, and the conflation of guardianship with content moderation are consistent with known debates in digital rights and child protection. However, the article relies heavily on opinion and interpretation without citing multiple independent sources or presenting counterarguments, which limits its journalistic reliability. The claims about lobbying and data profit are plausible but would require more detailed evidence and sourcing to be fully verified.
considerations: The article does not discuss the potential benefits or effectiveness of age verification in reducing actual harm to children online.
It overlooks how some regulatory frameworks balance privacy with child protection through technical and legal safeguards.
The role of technological innovation in creating less intrusive age verification methods is not addressed.
There is limited discussion on how local guardianship might be insufficient in certain contexts where children access harmful content without supervision.
The article does not consider perspectives from child protection advocates or policymakers who support age verification measures.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on skepticism about child protection laws being a guise for internet access control, surveillance, and corporate profit motives. Many commenters express concerns about privacy erosion, liability shifting, and the potential misuse of identity verification.

comments.author: SilverElfin
comments.text:
I read in some other discussions that this is about social media companies being able to increase their profits and nothing else. But the social media companies lobbying for these laws are shamelessly making it look like some kind of protect the children thing. It is all pushing more ads annd getting more users.

The way it works: today, social media companies cannot advertise to children under 13 under COPPA. So these companies have to do their best to guess the user’s age, and if it is possibly a child, they can’t advertise and have to lose those profits even though MAYBE the user is an adult. Now they can shift the legal compliance costs and liability to the operating system provider or phone manufacturer and not be responsible for the user’s identity. And then they can advertise much more at that point, without being conservative. This also lets them have a different experience for minors that doesn’t advertise to them, but targets them carefully to keep them as users until they are older, so they start to become a source of advertising profits later.

It’s well known that Meta is behind a lot of funding for nonprofits pushing these laws under a “protect the children” thing. But now even Pinterest’s CEO is shamelessly saying parents don’t have a responsibility to manage their own kids, and is supporting all of this. See https://www.gadgetreview.com/reddit-user-uncovers-who-is-beh... and https://time.com/article/2026/03/19/pinterest-ceo-government...

Evangelist/theocratic conservatives welcome these laws because they view it as enabling and validating age-based restrictions for other things. For example, Project 2025 called for a ban on porn. And separately, the Heritage Foundation pushed age-verification for porn websites, and has openly admitted it is a defacto porn ban. That should have been ruled unconstitutional on free speech grounds, but the current SCOTUS upheld it unfortunately. They’ll next use age-based verification for all sorts of content - maybe for LGBTQ stuff, maybe for something else.

In the end, everyone else will lose. If you have to prove your identity to anyone, there is a high chance this information can be accessed and surveilled by the government. There is a high chance at some point, no matter what they claim, your identity data will be hacked and sold. And of course if you can be identified online, then anything you say or do can be traced back to you, and that can be used against you by the government. Suddenly, being a protester in these chaotic times will become a lot more risky.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and nuanced explanation supported by references, accurately highlighting the economic incentives behind child protection legislation.

comments.author: borissk
comments.text:
The big tech is going to be one of the big winners from Internet Access Control. This will give them a more reliable way to link a user account to an actual human being - a link that can be monetized in a variety of ways. All kind of political regimes can use such regulations to enhance their control of the population. And the loosers are going to be the Internet users and small companies.

The unfortunate true is IAC is coming to most countries in the world, no matter how much the Hacker News audience hates it...
comments.analysis: The comment correctly identifies the monetization and control incentives for big tech and the likely global spread of such regulations, reflecting current trends.

comments.author: dlcarrier
comments.text: For the US, the worst of it started in 2019, when the held YouTube liable for all content that a child might access. (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YouTube_and_privacy#COPPA_sett...) That's what pushed all of the content networks to lobby for the liability to go somewhere else.
comments.analysis: This comment references a specific legal event (COPPA settlement) that influenced liability shifts, providing factual context to the discussion.

comments.author: bilekas
comments.text:
It's too late and never about children, simply deeper forms of data harvesting and surveillance.

What makes me extremely sad and concerned is that more recent generations simply have no idea or expectation of privacy online anymore. There will never be more of a fight against all this Orwellian behavior.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully connects child protection rhetoric to surveillance capitalism and societal privacy erosion, though somewhat speculative about generational attitudes.

comments.author: jjk166
comments.text: The people pushing for "child protection" went to the island. It's not even about control, it's about shifting liability away from platforms so they can further gut moderation, reducing their expenses and getting away with doing nothing to stop the actual bad actors.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights the economic incentives behind liability shifts, aligning with known industry practices, though less detailed than top-ranked comments.

comments.author: jameskilton
comments.text: That's the trick, it's always been about control. No-one in such positions actually cares about the children.
comments.analysis: While somewhat generalized and emotional, the comment reflects a common critical perspective on the motivations behind child protection policies.

comments.author: baal80spam
comments.analysis: This brief comment expresses a strong opinion but lacks elaboration or supporting arguments, reducing its analytical value.

comments.author: windowliker
comments.text: Arguments about erosion of privacy miss the point: that is exactly what they want.
comments.analysis: The comment succinctly captures a key point about privacy loss being a deliberate outcome, though it does not provide detailed evidence.

comments.author: jmcgough
comments.text: What's sad is how effective this is. Religious groups figured out a few years ago that anti-porn groups accomplish nothing, but if you start an anti-trafficking group you can restrict porn access.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a specific example of how child protection rhetoric is used to advance unrelated moral agendas, adding depth to the discussion.

comments.author: cat-turner
comments.text: parents need to do their job and raise their children, and moderate their content.
comments.analysis: This comment dismisses broader systemic issues and places responsibility solely on parents, lacking constructive engagement with the topic.
